regime_disagreement_note: Critical divergence: Claude sees disinflationary backdrop with sticky near-term inflation (3.16% YoY March CPI) but longer-term disinflation trajectory justifying zero Fed cuts. Gemini frames environment as inflationary with hawkish Fed response. This fundamental disagreement on economic direction should constrain position sizing and elevate skepticism toward consensus on Fed policy trades. Both models agree on imminent CPI data release as catalyst, but interpret its implications through opposite lenses.

single_source_trades.reason_other_missed: Gemini instead targets 3.4% threshold (YES at 13% edge) and 0.8% monthly (NO at 12% edge), creating range_mismatch on same event. Claude's 3.2% YoY threshold is more granular and better grounded in Cleveland Fed nowcast (3.16% actual). Gemini's 3.4% threshold is too high relative to nowcast data. Claude's specific source citation (Cleveland Fed nowcast 3.16%) provides stronger grounding than Gemini's general BLS reference.

Gemini trades broader 'rate cut before 2027' (any cut) at 9% edge (NO direction), which is directionally aligned but different contract scope. Claude's specific zero-cut contract (0.341 price, 15% edge) targets the most likely outcome per JPMorgan thesis. Gemini's wider-scope contract lacks the precision of Claude's explicit zero-cut focus. However, regime disagreement (disinflationary vs inflationary) means this trade carries elevated macro risk. Claude's JPMorgan source is explicit and recent; Gemini's Fed.gov reference is general.

contradictions.explanation: Both models agree direction (YES, i.e., inflation persists above threshold) but target different thresholds with non-overlapping confidence ranges. Claude bets CPI > 3.2% with 90% midpoint confidence; Gemini bets CPI > 3.4% with 30% midpoint confidence. Cleveland Fed nowcast of 3.16% YoY supports Claude's threshold more directly. Gemini's 3.4% threshold implies CPI would need to exceed nowcast by 24bp to resolve YES, which is the less likely scenario. Claude's range and catalyst grounding are stronger.

Claude YES on 'zero cuts in 2026' (KXRATECUTCOUNT-26DEC31-T0, 50% midpoint). Gemini NO on 'any rate cut before 2027' (0.6 midpoint probability of NO = 40% probability of YES). This is a directional disagreement on same underlying event, compounded by regime disagreement (Claude disinflationary, Gemini inflationary). Claude cites JPMorgan zero-cut thesis with resilient labor market rationale. Gemini cites hawkish Fed stance but lacks specific source. Both confidence levels medium. This is unresolvable without additional economic data; suggests skipping both or sizing very small pending CPI release and next Fed communication.

contradictions.market: NYC high temp below 53°F on April 9
contradictions.explanation: Python validator caught non-overlapping ranges misclassified as consensus

portfolio_concentration_warning: Regime disagreement (disinflationary vs inflationary) creates elevated macro model risk across inflation and fed_policy clusters. Total weight in macro-sensitive trades (fed_policy + inflation) is 4 relative_weight units. Recommend 60% max allocation to these clusters until CPI data on 2026-04-10 and next Fed communication resolve directional ambiguity. Weather trade (4 units) is uncorrelated macro hedge. If both inflation and fed_policy trades are sized, verify aggregate macro beta does not exceed risk appetite. Current proposed portfolio: 2 (CPI) + 2 (Fed cuts) + 4 (weather) = 8 relative weight units; suggests 8/(1-0.2) = 10 units of normalized bankroll exposure, within reasonable bounds if portfolio is $100K+ and 20% cash floor maintained.
reeval_triggers: March 2026 CPI data release (2026-04-10, 8:30 AM EST) — resolution of KXCPIYOY-26MAR-T3.2 will validate/invalidate Claude's 90% confidence thesis and likely shift market pricing of 2026 Fed cuts. Regimes may converge if actual CPI < 3.2%.

Next FOMC communications or Fed speakers on 2026 rate cut expectations — will clarify whether JPMorgan zero-cut thesis or market-implied modest cuts dominate. Currently Claude and Gemini diverge on Fed baseline.
Labor market data (April 2026 jobs report, weekly jobless claims) — both models identify labor market resilience as key to zero-cut scenario; deterioration would validate Gemini's cut risk.
Weather model update before 2026-04-09 12:00 PM EST — NYC temperature forecast confidence should increase as resolution approaches; if models shift significantly, reassess weather trade.
Geopolitical escalation or de-escalation related to Iran tensions — Claude cites as current Fed constraint; material shift would reopen rate cut probability.
